description: Training examples for continuous Q_f conservation laws in 2D/3D Euler equations.

examples.context: The Q_f = Σᵢ<ⱼ ΓᵢΓⱼ f(rᵢⱼ) conservation law for point vortices extends to continuous 2D Euler vorticity ω(x). The continuous analog Q_f[ω] is:
examples.truth: Q_f[ω] = ∫∫ ω(x)ω(y) f(|x-y|) dx dy, approximately conserved for smooth f

examples.context: For 2D Euler flow, when f(r) = -ln(r), the continuous Q_f gives:
examples.truth: Q_{-ln(r)}[ω] = kinetic energy (exact Casimir invariant)

examples.context: For 2D inviscid Euler vorticity ω, the quantity Q_{e^(-r)}[ω] = ∫∫ ω(x)ω(y) e^{-|x-y|} dx dy is:
examples.truth: approximately conserved with frac_var ~ 10⁻³ in numerical simulations

examples.context: For 2D Euler flow, Q_{√r}[ω] = ∫∫ ω(x)ω(y) √|x-y| dx dy is:
examples.truth: approximately conserved, extending the discrete Q_f family

examples.context: For the Q_f conservation law, why is the circulation-weighted form Q_f = Σ ΓᵢΓⱼ f(rᵢⱼ) better than the unweighted Σ f(rᵢⱼ)?
examples.truth: Circulation weighting gives 10000x better conservation (frac_var 10⁻⁵ vs 10⁻¹)

examples.context: For 3D Euler vorticity, vortex stretching affects Q_f conservation. Under stretching by factor s, Q_f scales as:
examples.truth: Q_f ∝ s² (stretching breaks Q_f conservation except for f(r) = 1/r which is energy)

examples.context: In 3D Euler, the kinetic energy corresponds to Q_{1/r}. Under vortex stretching, this quantity is:
examples.truth: exactly conserved (energy conservation is protected by the 1/r kernel)

examples.context: The ratio R_f = Q_{e^(-r)} / Q_{1/r} for 3D vortex dynamics is:
examples.truth: more stable than either Q_f alone (stretching factors partially cancel in the ratio)

examples.context: The Q_f conservation mechanism works because when ω(x)ω(y) is large (both points in vortex cores):
examples.truth: velocities u(x) ≈ u(y), so the advective derivative term nearly vanishes

examples.context: The 2D Euler enstrophy Z = ∫ ω² dx is:
examples.truth: exactly conserved (one of infinitely many Casimir invariants)

examples.context: For Navier-Stokes with viscosity ν, the Q_f quantities decay as:
examples.truth: dQ_f/dt ∝ -ν (linear in viscosity), returning to Euler conservation as ν → 0
distractors: Q_f is exact even with viscosity

examples.context: If Q_f bounds could control ||ω||_∞ growth in 3D, this would imply:
examples.truth: regularity of Navier-Stokes solutions (no finite-time blowup)

examples.context: The Q_f family for 2D Euler includes which special cases:
examples.truth: f(r) = -ln(r) gives energy, f(r) = r² gives moment of inertia, f(r) = e^(-r) is new

examples.context: The continuous Q_f = ∫∫ ω(x)ω(y) f(|x-y|) dx dy for 2D Euler was discovered:
examples.truth: as an extension of the discrete point-vortex Q_f = Σ ΓᵢΓⱼ f(rᵢⱼ)

examples.context: For 2D Euler with localized vorticity, Q_{tanh(r)}[ω] = ∫∫ ω(x)ω(y) tanh|x-y| dx dy is:
examples.truth: approximately conserved with frac_var ~ 10⁻² in turbulent simulations

examples.context: The Kirchhoff equations for point vortices are the special case of 2D Euler when:
examples.truth: vorticity is concentrated at point singularities ω = Σ Γᵢ δ(x - xᵢ)

examples.context: For 2D Euler, the stream function ψ satisfies ∇²ψ = -ω. The energy in terms of ψ is:
The 2D Euler energy E = -(1/2) ∫ ψω dx can be written as Q_f with f(r) =
examples.truth: -ln(r) / (2π) (the 2D Green's function)

examples.context: In 3D, the kinetic energy E = (1/2) ∫ |u|² dx can be written as:
examples.truth: E = (1/8π) ∫∫ ω(x)·ω(y) / |x-y| dx dy, which is Q_{1/r} (Biot-Savart)

examples.context: For 3D vortex rings undergoing stretching, the ratio R_f = Q_{e^(-r)} / Q_{1/r} shows:
examples.truth: frac_var ~ 10⁻² despite strong stretching (partial cancellation)

examples.context: The enstrophy Z = ∫ ω² dx in 3D is:
examples.truth: NOT conserved; it can grow without bound (vortex stretching amplifies ω)

examples.context: Why does enstrophy conservation fail in 3D but hold in 2D?
examples.truth: 3D vortex stretching ω·∇u amplifies |ω|; this term is absent in 2D

examples.context: For 2D turbulence, the inverse energy cascade is driven by:
examples.truth: conservation of both energy AND enstrophy constraining scale transfer

examples.context: The continuous Q_f conservation quality (frac_var) depends on:
examples.truth: the smoothness of f(r) and the coherence of vortex structures

examples.context: For point vortices, Q_f = Σᵢ<ⱼ ΓᵢΓⱼ f(rᵢⱼ) with f(r) = r is:
examples.truth: exactly conserved for vortex dipoles on the symmetry axis (frac_var ~ 10⁻¹⁵)

examples.context: The Q_f family conservation arises from:
examples.truth: the advective transport structure of Euler: dQ_f/dt involves ∇f·u which nearly cancels

examples.context: For 2D Euler, all functionals of the form ∫ F(ω) dx are:
examples.truth: Casimir invariants (conserved for any F, including enstrophy Z = ∫ ω² dx)

examples.context: The helicity H = ∫ u·ω dx in 3D ideal fluids is:
examples.truth: exactly conserved (topological invariant of vortex line linking)

examples.context: For the continuous Q_f to be well-defined, the kernel f(r) should be:
examples.truth: integrable or regularized near r=0 to avoid divergences

examples.context: The Q_f = ∫∫ ω(x)ω(y) f(|x-y|) dx dy changes under rescaling x → λx as:
examples.truth: Q_f → λ^(d-α) Q_f where α depends on the scaling of f(r)

examples.context: For numerical verification of Q_f conservation, a typical test case is:
examples.truth: Lamb-Oseen vortex pair or 2D turbulence with random initial vorticity

examples.context: The Q_f[ω] quantity for 2D Euler can be computed efficiently using:
examples.truth: FFT-based convolution since f(|x-y|) is a kernel

examples.context: For viscous Navier-Stokes, Q_f decays because:
examples.truth: viscous diffusion ν∇²ω causes vorticity spreading, reducing the double integral

examples.context: The frac_var metric for Q_f conservation quality is defined as:
examples.truth: frac_var = std(Q_f) / |mean(Q_f)| over the simulation time series

examples.context: For Q_f = ∫∫ ω(x)ω(y) f(|x-y|) dx dy, the time derivative dQ_f/dt involves:
examples.truth: ∫∫ ω(x)ω(y) f'(|x-y|)/|x-y| (r·(u(x)-u(y))) dx dy (advective transport)

examples.context: The Q_f conservation mechanism requires:
examples.truth: localized vorticity so that when ω(x)ω(y) is large, u(x) ≈ u(y)

examples.context: For 3D Euler with vortex stretching ω → s·ω (stretch factor s), Q_f transforms as:
examples.truth: Q_f → s² · Q_f (quadratic in stretch, except for Q_{1/r} = energy)

examples.context: The stretch-resistant ratio R_f = Q_{e^(-r)} / Q_{1/r} is useful because:
examples.truth: stretching affects both Q_f similarly, so the ratio partially cancels

examples.context: For 2D Euler, Q_{r²}[ω] = ∫∫ ω(x)ω(y) |x-y|² dx dy equals:
examples.truth: moment of inertia of vorticity distribution (exactly conserved)

examples.context: The discrete-to-continuous limit for Q_f as vortex blobs replace point vortices:
examples.truth: Q_f remains approximately conserved with frac_var scaling with blob smoothness
